meta.title: Regions & Communities
meta.subtitle: How Places Shape Daily Life • Printable Web Lesson

hero.ariaLabel: Children studying a world map to compare regions and communities
hero.imageQuery: children world map regions climates communities elementary geography hero

heroCopy.lede: Regions are areas with shared features. Land, water, climate, resources, and routes all shape how communities grow.
heroCopy.whatStudentsLearn: Students learn to describe regions by physical and human features, connect climate and resources to daily life, and plan routes or communities using geography evidence.
learningTargets: I can describe a region using shared features.
I can explain how climate affects daily life.
I can identify natural resources and careful use.
I can plan a community using land, water, and routes.
studentDirections: Read the section and study the image before answering.
Use the vocabulary and quick facts to support your thinking.
Write in complete sentences unless the prompt says bullets are okay.
On paper: circle, label, or underline important evidence in the print image.

keyVocabulary.def: An area with features that make it similar.
The usual weather in a place over a long time.
Something people use from the land, water, or environment.
A path people use to move from one place to another.

sections.imageQuery: aerial town roads homes physical human features region geography
sections.title: What Is a Region?
sections.lede: A region is an area with shared features. A region can be described by land, water, weather, plants, buildings, jobs, or culture.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: Some regions are hot and dry.
Some regions are cold and snowy.
Some regions are urban, rural, coastal, or mountainous.

cards.title: Look Closely
items: Notice land and water features.
Look for clues about homes or transportation.
Ask what makes this place similar to nearby places.

prompt.label: Prompt A — Describe a Region
prompt.placeholder: Describe a region using weather, land, plants, or buildings.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: A region can be described by shared physical or human features.

sections.ariaLabel: Aerial town view showing roads homes and land features that define a region

sections.imageQuery: desert mountains dry climate region geography
sections.title: Weather and Climate
sections.lede: Weather can change today, but climate describes what weather is usually like over a long time.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: Climate affects clothing and homes.
Climate affects which plants can grow.
Climate can affect transportation and outdoor activities.

cards.title: Compare
items: How might a snowy region be different from a desert?
What would people wear?
What would homes need to protect people from?

sections.callout: Climate is one clue. Geographers also study landforms, resources, and human choices.

prompt.label: Prompt B — Climate Clues
prompt.placeholder: How might daily life be different in a snowy region and a hot dry region?
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Climate affects clothing, homes, transportation, plants, and activities.

sections.ariaLabel: Desert mountain landscape showing a dry climate region

sections.imageQuery: forest river water trees natural resources geography
sections.title: Natural Resources
sections.lede: People use resources from land and water to meet needs. Communities do best when resources are used carefully.

cards.title: Vocabulary
items: Water can be used for drinking, farming, and transportation.
Soil helps people grow food.
Trees, fish, sunlight, and minerals are resources too.

cards.title: Resource Match
items: Name one resource in the image.
Tell how people might use it.
Explain one way to avoid wasting it.

prompt.label: Prompt C — Resource Match
prompt.placeholder: Choose one resource and explain how people use it carefully.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Resources include water, soil, trees, fish, minerals, and sunlight; careful use prevents waste.

sections.ariaLabel: Forest and river landscape showing water and trees as natural resources

sections.imageQuery: bridge road river transportation route geography
sections.title: Transportation and Routes
sections.lede: People choose routes based on land, water, distance, and safety. A smart route depends on the geography of a region.

cards.title: Examples
items: Roads may go around mountains.
Boats can move along rivers and coasts.
Bridges help people cross water.

cards.title: Think Like a Geographer
items: Find obstacles on the map.
Choose a safe route.
Explain why the route makes sense.

prompt.label: Prompt D — Plan a Route
prompt.placeholder: Pick a route across a region and explain why it is a smart path.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Student uses geography evidence such as rivers, mountains, roads, or distance.

sections.ariaLabel: Bridge and road crossing water to show transportation routes

sections.imageQuery: aerial community roads neighborhood planning geography
sections.title: Quick Check + Community Plan
sections.lede: Geography helps explain how communities grow, change, and solve problems.

cards.title: Mini Quiz
items: What is a region?
How can climate affect daily life?
Name one natural resource.

cards.title: Challenge
items: Design a small community.
Place homes, roads, water, and parks.
Explain why each feature belongs there.

promptE.label: Prompt E — Summary
promptE.placeholder: Summarize how land, water, climate, and resources shape communities.
promptE.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Summary connects physical features to human choices.

promptF.label: Prompt F — Create
promptF.placeholder: Design a small community and explain where homes, roads, water, and parks belong.
promptF.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Student places features logically and explains geography choices.

sections.ariaLabel: Aerial view of roads and buildings for planning a community
